sn47.7:1.1: This parable tells of a foolish monkey who gets stuck in a tar trap, reminiscent of “The Tarbaby and the Rabbit” story type. This has many instances worldwide. “Br’er Rabbit and the Tarbaby” was collected from stories told among black slaves in the US, and originates from Africa. Ja 55 tells of a warrior whose five weapons serve only to stick him to the coat of a yakkha. The Phillipine folk-tale Masoy and the Ape tells of an ape getting stuck in tar in the shape of a man. The current case is a little different, as there is no doll or mannikin.
sn47.7:1.4: The tar (lepa) was made from the sap of the banyan tree. | For oḍḍenti (“set snare”) see SN 1.67:1.1, SN 3.6:3.4, Ja 484:3.1. | I feel the text is deficient here: we do not know on what the tar was smeared, nor why the monkey would grab it. When, later, we are told it is a banana tree (though the reading is uncertain), these questions are answered. There, the pronoun tasmiṁyeva (“on that very”) implies that the banana tree had been mentioned before. I suspect it was mentioned here but has been lost.
sn47.7:2.12: The five points on which the monkey is stuck represent the five senses, trapping the monkey with greed.
sn47.7:2.13: In his note on this Sutta, Bodhi acknowledges the difficult reading kaṭṭha-kataṅgāre and proposes the emendation kaṭṭha-kaliṅgare based on the Buddhist Sanskrit kāṣṭha-kaḍambara. He then takes it to mean a block of wood, to which the hunter fastens the monkey before leaving with both monkey and block. This, however, creates several problems. Since both kaṭṭha and kaliṅgara mean “block of wood”, why the compound? Why does the hunter secure the monkey to the wood, when the whole point of the simile was that the monkey was stuck? Why read the verb avassajjetvā as “having fastened” when the normal meaning of avassajati is “release”, confirmed by the BJT reading uddharitvā, and agreeing with other occurrences of yena kāmaṁ pakkamati, which are preceded by someone being released (MN 66:10.1, AN 5.75:14.4, AN 8.14:7.1)? And why would the hunter carry a log of wood out from such a remote region? I propose that kaṭṭha-kataṅgāra is a corrupted form related to the Sanskrit kāṣṭha-kadalī, the wild plantain; perhaps the Musa mannii, a rare species found today in Arunchal Pradesh and sometimes known as the Indian Dwarf Banana. The hunter spears the trapped monkey while it is stuck to its favorite fruit tree, killing it, then pries it off and goes on his way.
